  consistency witness or immutable archived log peaks sufficient to prove every
  live grant/frontier from its certified checkpoint through the current root;
  key retirement/backup cannot drop required decoders, peaks or witness paths;
- model/tests cover unrelated appends, forged/non-minimal consistency paths,
  state-map-only inclusion, log/map mismatch, terminal transition omission,
  checkpoint fork/rollback, compaction witness corruption, key-epoch transition,
  proof resource exhaustion and completion CAS.

Verification:

- `cargo test -p sagnir-store`
- independent append-only consistency and state-map proof vectors;
- checkpoint/log/map/terminal-summary state-machine model;
- compaction/archive and adversarial proof-resource integration suite.

Exit criteria:

- Grant ancestry is established only by a bounded canonical append-only log
  consistency proof between complete checkpoints, never by state-map roots alone.
- Execution separately proves current exact `GrantReserved` leaf version and no
  conflicting terminal transition before journal mutation.
- Compaction preserves sufficient authenticated witness material for every live
  grant and admission frontier.

### v0.111.34 - Purpose-Separated Writer Admission Frontier Anchor

Goal: anchor maintenance admission frontiers without consuming or racing bootstrap
capsule or recovery-accounting anchor authority.

Deliverables:

- canonical `WriterAdmissionFrontierAnchor` capability descriptor declares exactly
  monotonic, external-CAS or witnessed mode, provider/witness identity, dedicated
  namespace/counter/CAS key, daemon/ledger scope, evidence format, durability,
  freshness, rate/capacity limits and assurance epoch;
- frontier anchor has purpose-separated keys, operation/idempotency IDs, provider
  namespace and policy authority; capsule/bootstrap, recovery-accounting and
  admission-frontier evidence fail cross-domain parsing and verification even when
  provider identity, numeric counter or commitment bytes coincide;
- prepared transition binds current `AdmissionLedgerCheckpoint`, complete
  `AdmissionFrontier`, expected prior frontier-anchor root/counter, maintenance
  grant/certificate, policy authorization and exact new anchor commitment;
- preparation, capacity reservation, provider/witness operation, ambiguity query
  and reconciliation complete before acquiring the journal writer/rotation lease;
  no external provider call or anchor retry runs while ordinary writers are excluded;
- ambiguous/conflicting frontier advancement blocks the associated maintenance
  operation and all later maintenance depending on that frontier, but already
  admitted ordinary writer tickets continue under the ledger and cannot be erased
  or delayed merely to force frontier convergence;
- expected-anchor CAS and predecessor linkage reject concurrent maintenance
  frontiers, skipped counters, split witnesses and same-epoch different roots;
  resolution never selects a frontier by timestamp, arrival or maintenance class;
- provider-capacity accounting reserves independent frontier-anchor operations,
  request rate, ambiguous slots and witness work before execution; policy batches
  frontiers/checkpoints and bounds maintenance frequency so it cannot exhaust or
  starve the bootstrap-anchor provider namespace or ordinary writer capacity;
- anchor key/provider rotation, backup, witness change and retirement preserve old
  checkpoint/frontier verification through the scheduling/rollback horizon and do
  not require a maintenance lease before new authority is usable;
- tests cover simultaneous capsule/recovery/frontier operations, cross-purpose
  evidence replay, provider shared-capacity exhaustion, response loss, CAS race,
  witness split view, maintenance cancellation and ordinary progress while frontier
  reconciliation is unavailable.

Verification:

- `cargo test -p sagnir-crypto`
- `cargo test -p sagnir-store`
- frontier-anchor capability/transition and scheduler state-machine models;
- provider-capacity, cross-domain and ambiguity integration suite.

Exit criteria:

- Maintenance exclusion starts only after its exact admission frontier is
  reconciled in a purpose-separated anchor namespace and the v0.111.37 bounded
  current-ledger satisfaction transaction succeeds immediately before lease CAS.
- Frontier ambiguity blocks maintenance without blocking already admitted ordinary
  writes or consuming bootstrap/recovery anchor counters.
- Cross-domain anchor evidence and shared-provider capacity cannot substitute
  authority or create an unbounded maintenance request path.

### v0.111.35 - Retention-Bound Recovery Copy Challenges

Goal: prove protected recovery copies remain immutable and retrievable from their
challenge through external anchor activation.

Deliverables:

- canonical `RecoveryCopyRetentionCapability` binds recovery-set/anchor transition,
  provider/account/credential, immutable object or version ID, exact byte length/
  digest, encryption-key dependency, retention generation, no-delete/governance-
  lock or CAS/lease semantics, start/expiry horizon and deletion-policy evidence;
- protected profiles accept only provider capabilities that prevent deletion,
  replacement, version aliasing and credential-scoped garbage collection from the
  successful restore challenge until the external anchor is reconciled plus the
  configured minimum post-activation recovery horizon;
- challenge reads the exact immutable version under a fresh unpredictable nonce,
  authenticates full committed bytes within v0.111.27/v0.111.30 budgets and binds
  response, provider receipt, retention capability and diversity descriptor into
  the prepared anchor transition;
- immediately after external preparation reconciliation and before availability
  activation, recovery obtains fresh provider evidence that the challenged version
  remains present and verifies an admitted v0.111.41 enforceable, independently
  attested or reviewed probabilistic retention mechanism; provider audit history
  may corroborate but cannot independently prove continuous retrievability, and a
  fresh metadata read alone proves only current presence;
- retention time/horizon uses admitted provider/authority epochs or signed lease
  generations, not local wall-clock reconstruction; expiry, revocation, provider
  clock conflict or unavailable freshness before activation refuses protected
  advancement rather than shortening the horizon;
- provider CAS/lease release, governance unlock, credential revocation and copy
  retirement are separate authorized operations permitted only after successor
  availability/retention evidence and the historical minimum horizon are satisfied;
- a provider unable to prevent or prove challenge/delete/replacement races is
  classified at most `ManagedRedundant`/`ManagedSingleFailureDomain` according to
  its other evidence and cannot contribute a `ProtectedDiverse` failure domain;
- post-activation unexpected deletion degrades current health and triggers bounded
  repair but does not rewrite the historical activation evidence; provider
  equivocation or a proof that retention was absent at activation enters conflict
  and invalidates the protected claim;
- tests cover delete immediately after challenge, same-name version replacement,
  lease expiry at each boundary, stale/cached proof, governance unlock, credential
  revocation, provider clock disagreement, anchor response loss, post-reconcile
  verification failure and successful multi-provider retained restore.

Verification:

- `cargo test -p sagnir-crypto`
- `cargo test -p sagnir-store`
- retention-capability/challenge/activation state-machine model;
- malicious provider race and lease/governance-lock integration suite.

Exit criteria:

- Every copy counted toward `ProtectedDiverse` has authenticated immutable
  retention from its successful challenge through anchor activation and the
  declared minimum horizon.
- Delete, replacement, expiry, cached-response or credential-revocation races
  refuse protected advancement or lower the copy's availability class.
- Historical evidence distinguishes verified retention at activation from later
  current-health degradation and repair.

### v0.111.36 - Daemon Recovery Bootstrap Token

Goal: locate the exact cold-recovery authority through a finite recipient-held
capability when all local daemon bootstrap bytes and identity are unavailable.

Deliverables:

- canonical `DaemonRecoveryBootstrapToken` is generated from the v0.23/v0.91
  production OS CSPRNG, has an unguessable versioned recovery namespace and is
  delivered to explicitly authorized recovery recipients/operators outside the
  daemon store before any transition relies on external cold recovery;
- token binds protocol/network version, provider and witness endpoint commitments,
  authenticated transport identities/pins, provider namespaces, daemon-scope
  commitment, anchor/checkpoint query key and exact latest-value query semantics;
  the daemon-scope commitment proves selection without exposing confidential daemon
  identity or permitting a provider namespace membership oracle;
- token also binds accounting-key locator commitment, expected recovery-root/
  transition fingerprint, admitted anchor/accounting suites and minimum accepted
  epochs so an endpoint cannot redirect recovery to another daemon or older root;
- hard cumulative maxima cover provider/witness endpoints, threshold participants,
  responses, redirects, handshakes, network/download bytes, signatures/proofs,
  provider calls, decode/crypto work, elapsed current-`ClockEpoch` work and temporary
  storage; before recovery accounting opens, attempts consume the token-bound
  v0.111.39 bootstrap discovery budget and later import its exact receipt;
- recovery authority is an explicit threshold/capability bound into the token and
  external transition; token possession alone does not bypass recipient signatures,
  hardware factors, policy epochs or quorum/diversity requirements;
- recovery queries only the token's finite committed endpoint set in canonical
  order or declared bounded parallelism and follows redirects only when their exact
  destination commitment is already in that set; it never enumerates accounts or
  provider namespaces, scans directories, performs wildcard queries, discovers
  arbitrary witnesses or infers daemon scope from local path/file names;
- each endpoint returns an authenticated latest-value statement, absence/refusal or
  bounded conflict proof for the exact query key; arrival order, provider-local
  timestamps and highest unbound counters cannot select authority, and split views
  remain explicit under the token's witness consistency/threshold rules;
- token rotation/supersession is a signed expected-prior transition binding old/new
  token commitments, endpoint sets, epochs and recovery roots; prior tokens remain
  usable only for bounded successor discovery and cannot authorize recovery after
  the declared retirement frontier;
- token backup/export uses v0.98.2 secret-delivery and possession-confirmation
  controls, supports multiple independent recipients and records no plaintext token
  in daemon logs, diagnostics or the sole local `.saga/` store; loss of all admitted
  token copies is an explicit potentially unrecoverable state, never an enumeration
  fallback;
- tests cover absent local capsule/descriptor/daemon identity, wrong token,
  namespace guessing, endpoint enumeration, open/cyclic redirect, transport-key
  substitution, split witnesses, threshold failure, old-token replay, rotation,
  recipient loss, budget exhaustion and successful finite cold recovery.

Verification:

- `cargo test -p sagnir-crypto`
- `cargo test -p sagnir-store`
- token format/query/supersession and cold-recovery state-machine models;
- malicious endpoint/redirect, threshold and bounded-resource integration suite.

Exit criteria:

- A valid recipient-held token deterministically locates and authenticates the
  finite latest external recovery authority without any local daemon identity.
- Missing/invalid/exhausted tokens refuse with an explicit recovery state and never
  trigger namespace/account/directory enumeration or arbitrary redirects.
- Token rotation, backup and loss have durable non-oracular semantics and cannot
  select a different daemon, older root or weaker recovery policy.

### v0.111.37 - Post-Anchor Admission Frontier Satisfaction

Goal: revalidate an externally reconciled maintenance frontier against the exact
current admission ledger immediately before acquiring writer exclusion.

Deliverables:

- after v0.111.34 external reconciliation and before journal writer/rotation lease
  acquisition, the scheduler enters one bounded admission-lock transaction with
  the frontier-anchor evidence, maintenance grant/certificate and expected
  admission/maintenance epochs fixed as inputs;
- transaction verifies a v0.111.33 append-only consistency proof that the externally
  anchored frontier checkpoint is a prefix of the current complete admission
  checkpoint; fork, truncation, log/map mismatch or proof limit exhaustion refuses
  this maintenance attempt without requesting another external transition;
- authenticated range/terminal/exception proofs account for every ordinary ticket
  at or below the frontier cutoff as transaction-committed/terminal, owner of the
  durable v0.111.40 next-writer handoff, represented by exact unconsumed finite
  priority debt, or independently authorized terminal cancellation/supersession;
  `GrantReserved` alone and any unaccounted earlier priority block maintenance;
- maintenance grant leaf, certificate, authorization, policy epoch, frontier anchor
  and expected maintenance epoch are reverified against current state; a completed,
  cancelled, superseded, replayed or competing maintenance activation refuses;
- successful transaction appends canonical `AdmissionFrontierSatisfied` binding
  anchored frontier, current admission checkpoint/log/map roots, cutoff accounting,
  maintenance grant/certificate, exact ordinary grant/debt set, writer-lease
  generation and predecessor satisfaction record, then publishes its checkpoint;
- the writer-lease CAS immediately consumes that exact satisfaction record and
  expected current checkpoint/maintenance epoch; any intervening relevant ledger
  transition, competing maintenance CAS or already active ordinary writer makes
  the CAS stale and requires bounded local revalidation, never anchor reuse by
  assumption or a second external anchor operation for unrelated later tickets;
- ordinary tickets above the anchored cutoff may append concurrently and follow the
  declared post-frontier fairness policy; they do not invalidate the anchor, while
  an unsatisfied ticket at/below cutoff blocks that maintenance attempt until it is
  terminal, executes under its grant or becomes authorized finite priority debt;
- admission lock holds only bounded proof verification, authenticated map/log
  update and satisfaction append; no provider/witness call, journal payload,
  migration, key operation or writer critical section occurs under it;
- anchor ambiguity continues to block maintenance but not admitted ordinary writes;
  satisfaction failure preserves all queue/debt state and emits no maintenance
  success, and cancellation cleans only its own non-authoritative attempt state;
- tests cover ordinary append during external reconciliation, earlier/later ticket
  changes, terminal/debt proof omission, grant replay, competing maintenance epoch,
  state change between satisfaction and lease CAS, crash at each transaction/CAS
  boundary and eventual maintenance under sustained bounded ordinary load.

Verification:

- `cargo test -p sagnir-store`
- frontier-satisfaction/lease-CAS scheduler state-machine model;
- consistency/range/terminal proof and concurrent admission integration suite;
- crash-at-every-boundary and sustained fairness tests.

Exit criteria:

- Writer exclusion consumes an exact current-checkpoint satisfaction record proving
  every pre-cutoff ordinary priority is accounted for after external reconciliation.
- Unrelated post-cutoff tickets do not require another external anchor operation,
  but one unsatisfied earlier ticket or competing maintenance epoch blocks the CAS.
- No provider operation or unbounded proof work occurs under admission or writer
  locks, and failure leaves ordinary progress and queue authority intact.

### v0.111.38 - Two-Stage Recovery Availability Activation

Goal: prevent an external transition from asserting protected availability before
continuous retention through the activation boundary has been proven.

Deliverables:

- external authority uses two domain-separated predecessor-linked transitions:
  `RecoveryAvailabilityPrepared` commits candidate recovery set, exact immutable
  versions, retention capabilities, restore challenges, intended class, accounting
  root/budget and policy, but explicitly carries `AvailabilityPending` and makes no
  historical `ProtectedDiverse` assertion;
- first expected-state CAS/reconciled witness transition places the candidate in a
  recoverable pending state while retaining prior active availability authority;
  timeout/response loss queries that exact operation and never treats prepared or
  locally finalized metadata as activated availability;
- after preparation reconciliation, fresh evidence proves every required retention
  lease/governance lock remains valid beyond the proposed activation horizon and
  that the challenged immutable version is still the retained object;
- continuous-retention proof must derive from enforceable no-delete/version-lock
  semantics, independently attested storage enforcement or a reviewed proof-of-
  retrievability/data-possession construction under v0.111.41; provider audit
  history can corroborate such evidence but never independently establishes
  retrievability, and a current metadata read proves only one instant;
- canonical `RecoveryAvailabilityActivated` binds prepared transition, fresh
  retention/continuity evidence root, final copy/diversity evaluation, exact final
  availability class, activation horizon, expected pending anchor state and current
  accounting root, then performs a second external expected-state CAS or witnessed
  transition before local finalization reports the class;
- activation response loss reconciles only the exact second operation; same pending
  state with conflicting evidence/class, expired capability, deleted/replaced copy,
  accounting exhaustion or external CAS conflict remains `AvailabilityPending`/
  `RecoveryRequired` and never records a historical protected claim;
- policy may explicitly abandon pending state or prepare a lower class through a
  new signed expected-state transition; failure never silently downgrades, rewrites
  the intended class or reuses challenge/retention evidence beyond freshness;
- copy retention/release horizons begin no later than challenge and extend through
  successful activation plus policy minimum; cleanup, key retirement, governance
  unlock and credential revocation cannot run merely because preparation committed;
- status and proofs distinguish prior active class, candidate intended class,
  pending recoverability, final activated class and current post-activation health
  without exposing hidden copy topology or claiming global availability early;
- tests cover crash/response loss at both external operations, delete/replace after
  preparation, expired lease, forged/gapped provider audit history, fresh metadata-
  only evidence, conflicting second CAS, abandonment/lower-class reproposal,
  accounting exhaustion and successful protected activation.

Verification:

- `cargo test -p sagnir-crypto`
- `cargo test -p sagnir-store`
- two-stage availability/retention/accounting state-machine model;
- provider continuity-proof and dual-CAS/witness integration suite.

Exit criteria:

- Preparation creates only recoverable pending authority and never a historical
  protected availability claim.
- `ProtectedDiverse` becomes authoritative only after a second expected-state
  transition binds fresh enforceable continuous-retention and diversity evidence.
- Failed, expired, ambiguous or conflicting final verification remains pending or
  recovery-required without implicit activation or downgrade.

### v0.111.39 - Pre-Accounting Bootstrap Discovery Budget

Goal: bound and preserve cold-discovery work before the recovered accounting key
can authenticate normal anchor-recovery accounting.

Deliverables:

- canonical `BootstrapDiscoveryBudget` is committed directly by each
  `DaemonRecoveryBootstrapToken` and binds token/daemon-scope commitment, budget
  epoch, fixed endpoint set/order, threshold policy and checked maxima for endpoint
  queries, redirects, responses, bytes, signatures/proofs, handshakes, provider/
  witness calls, decode/crypto work, temporary storage and current-process duration;
- discovery begins with a random CSPRNG `DiscoveryAttemptId` and domain-separated
  idempotency key bound to token generation, query key, expected recovery-root
  fingerprint, endpoint and exact requested latest-value operation;
- provider/witness endpoints maintain a purpose-specific operation journal,
  monotonic use counter or admitted hardware-backed equivalent keyed by token
  generation/budget epoch; v0.111.42 admits either one externally coordinated
  global counter or non-overlapping endpoint allocations, so independent endpoints,
  fresh attempt IDs, restart, recipient retry or clones cannot multiply capacity;
- endpoint operation evidence records reservation, execution, exact bounded result
  class and consumed counters without exposing confidential daemon identity; an
  ambiguous result remains charged at the maximum reservation until the exact
  idempotent operation is reconciled;
- recovery recipients retain authenticated `BootstrapDiscoveryReceipt` records for
  every endpoint attempt, including absence/refusal/conflict, request/response
  commitments, provider counter, consumed budgets and predecessor receipt root;
  local receipt loss does not permit a new provider-side attempt under the same key;
- state machine is `DiscoveryAuthorized -> QueryPending -> DiscoveryReceipted ->
  AccountingImported`, with explicit `Ambiguous`, `Exhausted` and `Conflict`; no
  `AnchorRecoveryAccountingEpoch` operation, reset or refill is invoked before the
  exact accounting key/root has been recovered and verified;
- opening recovery accounting atomically imports token commitment, discovery attempt,
  provider journal/use-counter evidence, complete receipt root and consumed work
  into the first resumed `AnchorRecoveryAccountingEpoch` transition before any
  managed-copy query; duplicate, partial, reordered or cross-token import refuses;
- imported discovery work consumes the corresponding recovery resource classes and
  cannot be refunded, imported twice or hidden by a larger normal recovery budget;
  subsequent endpoint/copy work uses only the authenticated recovery accounting;
- providers without durable idempotent attempt tracking are admitted only under an
  explicit weaker profile that pre-authorizes one fixed endpoint operation per
  token generation and cannot retry after ambiguous completion, or refuses cold
  discovery where policy requires restart-abuse resistance;
- crash/response-loss tests cover every state before accounting-key availability,
  provider committed/local receipt missing, local receipt written/provider response
  lost, restart/clone/new-attempt-ID churn, one-attempt weaker profile, import crash,
  duplicate import, counter rollback, budget exhaustion and successful handoff.

Verification:

- `cargo test -p sagnir-crypto`
- `cargo test -p sagnir-store`
- bootstrap-discovery budget/receipt/import state-machine model;
- provider idempotency/use-counter and crash/restart integration suite.

Exit criteria:

- Every pre-accounting endpoint operation consumes a token-bound finite budget that
  restart, response loss, receipt loss or clone cannot reset.
- Recovery accounting is unused before key/root verification and atomically imports
  the complete discovery receipt before any managed-copy operation.
- Providers lacking durable attempt tracking expose an explicit one-attempt/weaker
  guarantee and never gain silent retry capacity.

### v0.111.40 - Durable Ordinary Next-Writer Handoff

Goal: ensure a pre-cutoff ordinary writer that has only been granted cannot be
overtaken by maintenance before its transaction or priority is resolved.

Deliverables:

- canonical `OrdinaryWriterHandoff` binds admission ledger/checkpoint, ticket ID/
  leaf version, grant certificate/generation, journal transaction/idempotency key,
  expected writer-lease generation, principal/capability commitment, payload or
  deterministic reconstruction commitment, v0.111.45 durable staged-payload
  commitment, handoff sequence and predecessor handoff root;
- scheduler creates at most one durable next-writer handoff through admission-ledger
  CAS after `GrantReserved` only for a fully staged resumable payload; an explicit
  non-resumable ticket can queue but never owns a handoff; states are `Reserved`,
  `LeaseClaimed`,
  `TransactionCommitted`, `ConvertedToPriorityDebt`, `TerminalCancelled` and
  `Conflict`, with no reusable or arrival-order-selected state;
- writer-lease CAS gives precedence to the exact current ordinary handoff and
  rejects every maintenance lease while that handoff is `Reserved` or
  `LeaseClaimed`; a grant certificate without the durable handoff never counts as
  satisfied frontier priority and cannot authorize a maintenance bypass;
- the ordinary writer claims the exact handoff/lease generation, executes one
  idempotent journal transaction and atomically binds its committed result back to
  the admission leaf/handoff; response loss reconciles that transaction before
  either writer class can advance the handoff;
- disconnect, process death or unavailable staged payload cannot silently cancel
  or erase lease priority; bounded recovery either resumes the exact immutable
  payload/reconstruction material or performs one independently authorized conversion to
  unconsumed finite priority debt before maintenance can satisfy the cutoff; if
  both paths are unavailable, v0.111.43 enters typed recovery-required state rather
  than claiming bounded recovery necessarily completes;
- independently authorized cancellation/supersession binds ticket, handoff, reason,
  authority/policy epoch and proof no journal result exists; a local disconnect,
  timeout, maintenance urgency or caller receipt is insufficient authority;
- `AdmissionFrontierSatisfied` accepts each pre-cutoff ordinary ticket only when
  its transaction is committed/terminal, it owns the exact current handoff whose
  writer lease must run before maintenance, it has unconsumed priority debt, or it
  has an independently authorized terminal cancellation/supersession;
- if a handoff remains runnable, frontier satisfaction may record it as accounted
  but the maintenance writer-lease CAS must fail until the handoff transaction
  commits or an authorized debt/terminal transition produces a new checkpoint and
  bounded satisfaction revalidation;
- handoff compaction/backup retains every live or ambiguous handoff, terminal
  result/debt binding and exact lease-generation evidence; restart reconstructs the
  next handoff before granting any writer or maintenance lease;
- model/tests cover grant before handoff, grant-before-lease crash, disconnected
  grantee, payload loss, ordinary/maintenance simultaneous CAS, response loss after
  journal commit, handoff-to-debt conversion, cancellation forgery, restart and
  maintenance-overtake attempts.

Verification:

- `cargo test -p sagnir-store`
- ordinary handoff/writer-lease/frontier state-machine model;
- response-loss, disconnect, restart and concurrent-CAS integration suite;
- sustained ordinary/maintenance fairness tests.

Exit criteria:

- `GrantReserved` alone always blocks maintenance frontier satisfaction.
- A live ordinary handoff either receives the next writer lease or transitions
  through independently authorized terminal/debt authority before maintenance.
- Maintenance cannot overtake an earlier ordinary grant across crash, disconnect,
  response loss or competing lease CAS.

### v0.111.41 - Retention Assurance Mechanisms And Policy

Goal: distinguish enforceable or independently verifiable retrievability from
provider assertions when activating protected recovery availability.

Deliverables:

- canonical `RetentionAssuranceDescriptor` classifies each copy's evidence as
  `EnforcedRetention`, `IndependentlyAttestedStorage`, `ProbabilisticRetrievability`
  or `ProviderAsserted`, bound to copy/version, provider/failure domain, mechanism/
  parameter revision, verifier roots, challenge schedule, horizon and evidence root;
- `EnforcedRetention` requires governance/no-delete lock below the mutable account
  plane or provider-enforced immutable-object/version retention whose authorization,
  expiry and deletion semantics are independently authenticated and cannot be
  shortened by the ordinary account credential;
- `IndependentlyAttestedStorage` requires admitted hardware or independent storage-
  enforcement attestation with measured component identity, trusted verifier/root,
  freshness, rollback/revocation state, object/version commitment and declared
  physical/storage assurance; provider self-attestation remains provider-asserted;
- `ProbabilisticRetrievability` uses a separately reviewed proof-of-retrievability
  or proof-of-data-possession construction with domain-separated unpredictable
  challenges, authenticated object encoding/metadata, sample count, adversarial
  model, extraction/soundness bound, verifier key lifecycle and exact probabilistic
  guarantee; v0.111.44 binds that metadata to exact ciphertext-copy lifecycle and
  privacy rules, and no sampling claim becomes deterministic continuous storage;
- challenge randomness is generated after the copy/version/commitment is fixed,
  cannot be provider-selected or replayed, and uses the v0.23/v0.91 entropy boundary;
  routine response verification and activation assurance are bounded and charged
  under v0.111.47 retention-verification accounting, not cold-recovery accounting;
- authenticated append-only provider audit history, current metadata, receipts and
  self-reported retention flags are corroborating `ProviderAsserted` evidence only;
  they may expose contradiction/equivocation but cannot independently establish
  uninterrupted storage, retrievability or a protected failure domain;
- policy declares admitted mechanism classes, minimum attestation independence,
  probabilistic soundness threshold/challenge cadence, retention horizon, mechanism
  diversity and common-mode dependencies; `ProviderAsserted` never contributes a
  copy or failure domain toward `ProtectedDiverse`;
- v0.111.38 activation binds each copy's assurance descriptor and verifies fresh
  evidence spanning preparation through activation; an expired/revoked attestation,
  failed/missed challenge, weakened parameters or mechanism downgrade remains
  pending/recovery-required and cannot be replaced by provider audit entries;
- status/proofs state whether the claim is enforced, independently attested,
  probabilistic or provider-asserted and disclose probabilistic bounds without
  revealing hidden topology; historical evidence preserves the exact mechanism/
  parameters while current health records later failure or revalidation;
- tests cover forged provider audit history, bytes deleted while logs claim present,
  account-plane lock bypass, mutable-version alias, self-attestation, stale/revoked
  attestation, predictable/replayed challenge, insufficient samples, invalid PoR/
  PoDP response, mechanism downgrade and mixed-assurance diversity policy.

Verification:

- `cargo test -p sagnir-crypto`
- `cargo test -p sagnir-store`
- independent retention-assurance format and challenge vectors;
- assurance-policy/two-stage-activation state-machine model;
- malicious provider, attestation and probabilistic benchmark suite.

Exit criteria:

- Provider assertions and audit history alone never count toward
  `ProtectedDiverse` or claim continuous retrievability.
- Every protected copy has an admitted enforceable, independently attested or
  reviewed probabilistic mechanism with exact fresh evidence and honest bounds.
- Policy rejects expired, weakened, replayed or cross-mechanism evidence rather
  than silently downgrading protected availability.

### v0.111.42 - Coordinated Discovery Budget And Pre-Authentication Gate

Goal: prevent independent recovery endpoints or unauthenticated callers from
multiplying or exhausting legitimate bootstrap discovery capacity.

Deliverables:

- token selects exactly one canonical `BootstrapDiscoveryBudgetMode`:
  `ExternallyCoordinated` or `StaticallyPartitioned`; mixed, omitted, unknown or
  dynamically inferred modes refuse before a legitimate capacity reservation;
- `ExternallyCoordinated` binds one coordinator capability/namespace, CAS or
  consensus/threshold counter semantics, participant identities, consistency/
  split-view rules, global maxima, operation format and availability assurance;
  every endpoint operation first consumes one exact coordinator reservation and
  later reconciles result/refund without another independent endpoint allowance;
- coordinator timeout/lost response is one idempotent pending operation charged at
  maximum until exact query reconciliation; a local counter, timestamp, individual
  endpoint view or threshold share cannot replace the globally consistent result;
- `StaticallyPartitioned` commits a non-overlapping allocation for each endpoint:
  endpoint/transport identity, query/response/redirect/byte/signature/handshake/
  decode/crypto/provider-work allowances, attempt-ID prefix/range, maximum parallel
  reservations and allocation generation; each endpoint enforces only its slice;
- partition ranges and counters are canonical, disjoint and checked for overlap/
  overflow before token activation; endpoint/recipient/threshold parallelism cannot
  reserve the same slice twice, and unused capacity is non-transferable without a
  signed successor token that supersedes old allocations before new use;
- threshold/recovery-recipient authorization is verified before coordinator or
  endpoint legitimate-budget reservation; partial threshold, wrong hardware factor,
  invalid signature, revoked recipient or token-only possession consumes no valid
  recovery allowance and reveals no token/daemon validity oracle;
- invalid/unauthenticated traffic is handled under a separate provider-local public
  abuse budget with hard source/endpoint/global rate, byte, handshake and crypto-
  work limits; abuse exhaustion may refuse public traffic but cannot decrement,
  lock or advance the authenticated token's recovery budget state;
- authorization transcripts bind token generation, endpoint allocation/coordinator,
  threshold participant set, one discovery operation and expected query key;
  parallel participants contribute to one threshold authorization rather than each
  creating a separately chargeable legitimate reservation;
- all coordinator/partition receipts feed the v0.111.39 discovery receipt root and
  later accounting import; contradictory endpoint/coordinator counters, allocation-
  range reuse or valid-authority-after-abuse refusal enters conflict/availability
  failure without falling back to another budget model;
- models/tests cover simultaneous recipients across all endpoints, parallel
  threshold shares, same allocation attempt-range races, coordinator CAS conflict/
  partition, endpoint response loss, unused-slice transfer attempt, stolen token,
  invalid-auth flood, public-budget exhaustion and complete receipt import.

Verification:

- `cargo test -p sagnir-crypto`
- `cargo test -p sagnir-store`
- coordinator/partition allocation and pre-auth gate state-machine models;
- multi-recipient/endpoint/threshold concurrency and abuse integration suite.

Exit criteria:

- One token uses either globally coordinated compare-and-consume or immutable
  disjoint endpoint allowances; no independent endpoint can grant the full total.
- Invalid or token-only requests cannot consume legitimate discovery capacity, and
  public abuse limits cannot mutate authenticated recovery budget state.
- Parallel recipients and threshold participants produce one bounded reservation
  history whose exact receipts import into recovery accounting.

### v0.111.43 - Writer Handoff Recovery Epoch And Blocking State

Goal: bound ordinary handoff recovery across restart and report honestly when
neither payload/result recovery nor independent priority conversion is available.

Deliverables:

- canonical `WriterHandoffRecoveryEpoch` binds daemon/ledger/checkpoint, handoff/
  ticket/grant/journal operation, recovery generation, process `ClockEpoch`, prior
  recovery root, independent debt/cancellation authority and policy limits;
- checked persistent maxima cover payload-resume attempts, journal-result queries,
  provider calls, response/temporary bytes, decode/hash/crypto work, concurrent
  operations and failures; each reservation is charged before execution and
  overflow/refusal cannot mutate handoff priority;
- local elapsed/critical duration is compared only within the current process
  `ClockEpoch`; restart closes in-flight work with conservative maximum charges and
  starts no fresh persistent allowance, while wall time, disconnect duration,
  process identity and local file age never expire a handoff or authorize debt;
- journal-result reconciliation uses the exact v0.111.26/v0.111.40 transaction and
  idempotency key; response loss queries that operation, and committed result is
  bound atomically to handoff/ticket before any lease or debt transition proceeds;
- payload recovery resumes only the authenticated payload/resume commitment under
  fixed byte/work bounds; substituted, missing, non-resumable or exhausted payload
  cannot be guessed, regenerated or treated as cancellation;
- debt/cancellation conversion requires the independent current authority and exact
  no-result proof; authority timeout/unavailability, policy conflict or response
  ambiguity remains charged and cannot be replaced by local administrator intent,
  disconnect, wall time or maintenance urgency;
- when payload/result recovery cannot proceed and independent conversion authority
  is unavailable, conflicted or exhausted, state becomes typed
  `WriterHandoffRecoveryRequired` with immutable blocked-handoff/root evidence;
- in `WriterHandoffRecoveryRequired`, the blocked handoff retains next-writer
  priority: all later ordinary and maintenance writer-lease acquisitions stop,
  already queued tickets remain charged/ordered, new writer admission refuses once
  the bounded queue policy requires it, and read-only/non-authority operations plus
  the independent handoff-recovery path remain available;
- no lower-assurance profile may silently overtake the blocked handoff; an explicit
  v0.111.46 emergency incarnation recovery preserves permanent evidence and fences
  late results under independent threshold authority; it is not a scheduling bypass;
- recovery resumes from the exact authenticated epoch when payload/provider/
  authority returns, reconciles result or converts to debt/terminal state, then
  publishes a new ledger checkpoint before later lease scheduling restarts;
- status exposes a coarse non-oracular blocked/recovery-required condition and the
  documented safety-versus-availability limitation without leaking payload,
  principal, exact queue position or provider timing;
- tests cover every limit, response loss, restart/clone, cross-`ClockEpoch` duration,
  missing/non-resumable payload, unavailable/conflicting debt authority, blocked
  later ordinary/maintenance leases, bounded new-admission refusal, authority
  restoration and successful ordered continuation.

Verification:

- `cargo test -p sagnir-store`
- handoff recovery/accounting/authority state-machine model;
- process-kill, provider-loss and blocked-queue integration suite;
- resource-limit and safety-versus-availability tests.

Exit criteria:

- Handoff recovery has persistent checked attempt/work/provider budgets that restart
  cannot reset and never derives authority from wall time or disconnect.
- Unavailable payload/result and conversion authority produce explicit
  `WriterHandoffRecoveryRequired`; no later writer or maintenance lease overtakes it.
- Read-only and independent recovery operations remain usable, and restored
  authority resumes exact queue order without invented results or silent bypass.

### v0.111.44 - Private Retrievability Metadata Migration

Goal: bind PoR/PoDP evidence to exact encrypted recovery-copy versions while
preserving privacy and authority across re-encryption, relocation and key rotation.

Deliverables:

- canonical `RetrievabilityMetadataManifest` binds recovery copy/endpoint,
  logical copy commitment, exact ciphertext storage/version ID, ciphertext root/
  length, authenticated placement context, encryption/retention/availability epochs,
  assurance construction/parameter revision, tag root/count, verifier-key epoch,
  predecessor manifest and migration operation;
- proof tags/metadata and verifier transcripts expose no plaintext commitment,
  content equality, path/name, compartment, object count/topology or private
  locator through public/blind-store metadata; required public fields are fixed/
  padded opaque commitments, while sensitive tags and mappings are encrypted under
  purpose-specific metadata keys with declared observer leakage;
- verifier/tag keys are purpose-separated from content encryption, private-ID,
  migration, recovery-accounting, receipt and signing keys; derivation domains,
  rotation authority, provider assurance and compromise/retirement are independent;
- changing ciphertext bytes, authenticated layout, encoding, tag construction,
  verifier key or proof parameter creates a copy-on-write metadata/ciphertext
  generation; tags are recomputed for the exact new stored ciphertext and cannot
  be copied, patched or replayed from another copy/version;
- old ciphertext/metadata/version remains active, challengeable and retained until
  the new ciphertext, complete tags, manifest, verifier key, full restore challenge
  and v0.111.38 availability activation all verify and atomically supersede it;
  cleanup/old-key retirement follows retention and rollback horizons afterward;
- relocation is byte-for-byte unchanged only when ciphertext/version identity and
  every proof-authenticated placement coordinate/context remain stable; otherwise
  it is a new copy-on-write proof generation even if ciphertext bytes are equal;
- challenge pins one exact active manifest/ciphertext/tag/verifier-key generation;
  concurrent re-encryption may prepare another generation but cannot mix old/new
  tags, answer from a substituted copy or activate while the pinned challenge is
  unresolved;
- metadata migration manifest binds complete expected tag range/count/root and
  work/byte budgets; missing, duplicated, reordered, corrupt or mixed-generation
  tags, partial writes and provider response ambiguity remain non-authoritative and
  quota-charged until exact reconciliation or authenticated cleanup;
- verifier-key rotation stages new key/tags/ciphertext binding under independent
  authority, preserves old verification through active/rollback horizons and uses
  one expected-manifest CAS; key loss, wrong epoch or rotation crash cannot select
  a mixed proof set or silently lower assurance to provider assertion;
- backup, repair, partial clone and sync carry manifest/version/tag completeness and
  privacy labels; blind endpoints cannot correlate copies through reusable tags,
  and unavailable proof metadata returns typed unavailable/pending rather than
  accepting ciphertext presence as retrievability evidence;
- tests cover re-encryption during challenge, tag/ciphertext/version substitution,
  cross-copy and cross-compartment tag replay, partial/corrupt tag migration,
  placement-context change, verifier-key rotation at every crash boundary, mixed
  backup restore, blind-store correlation and old/new activation/retirement.

Verification:

- `cargo test -p sagnir-crypto`
- `cargo test -p sagnir-store`
- retrievability manifest/tag/key canonical and privacy vectors;
- copy-on-write proof-migration/activation state-machine model;
- challenge/re-encryption/relocation/key-rotation fault suite.

Exit criteria:

- Every retrievability proof identifies one exact ciphertext copy/version and
  placement context without exposing plaintext membership or topology.
- Ciphertext/proof changes activate only as a complete copy-on-write generation;
  old evidence remains valid until new restore and availability activation finish.
- Mixed, partial, replayed, corrupt or wrong-key proof metadata never establishes
  protected availability or permits premature old-version retirement.

### v0.111.45 - Durable Handoff Payload Staging Gate

Goal: prevent caller loss or deliberate disconnect from turning a non-resumable
request into a next-writer handoff that can freeze all later writes.

Deliverables:

- canonical `HandoffPayloadStaged` binds ticket/grant/operation, complete request
  payload or deterministic reconstruction material, schema/decoder revision,
  exact plaintext length/digest commitment, encrypted representation root/length,
  staging/key/policy epochs, resource charge and predecessor staging commitment;
- payload/reconstruction material is canonical and sufficient for a fresh process
  to execute the exact idempotent journal request without caller memory, live file
  descriptors, mutable worktree state, network refetch or interpretation of an
  untrusted resume hint;
- staging uses purpose-separated encryption/HMAC keys and nonce domains, opaque
  owner-only names and immutable generation files; debug/status/log output exposes
  no payload, digest, path or principal beyond the selected privacy profile;
- content confidentiality does not imply traffic privacy: v0.111.51 is the mandatory
  closure for storage-observer size/timing/timestamp/backup and cleanup claims before
  protected handoff staging can activate;
- durable byte, object, decode/work, crypto and temporary-storage quota reservation
  precedes file creation and remains charged across short write, cancellation,
  ambiguity, process death, key loss and cleanup until exact reconciliation;
- safe publication writes one exclusive temporary file, authenticates/rereads exact
  bytes, synchronizes file data/metadata, atomically publishes the immutable staging
  generation and synchronizes its parent directory under the admitted platform
  durability profile before any handoff CAS can observe it;
- handoff creation verifies staging commitment, key availability, retention horizon,
  policy epoch, operation ID and complete quota charge in the same bounded admission-
  ledger transaction; stale, missing, mutable, partially durable or wrong-operation
  staging refuses without granting next-writer priority;
- recovery keys/wrappers and decoder/schema remain available independently of the
  caller/process for the maximum handoff/recovery horizon; rotation/migration keeps
  old staged generations usable until exact handoff/result completion or authorized
  debt/cancellation transition;
- an explicit non-resumable ticket may remain queued without a handoff, be
  independently cancelled/superseded, or be converted into finite priority debt by
  admitted authority, but can never enter `OrdinaryWriterHandoff::Reserved` or own
  the next writer-lease generation;
- cleanup occurs only after journal transaction/result and admission-ledger handoff/
  ticket completion reconcile, or after independently authorized no-result debt/
  terminal transition; disconnect, local timeout or staging age never deletes it;
- tests cover caller disconnect before/during/after staging, file/parent sync crash,
  replaced/symlinked staging, quota exhaustion, wrong key/schema/policy/operation,
  non-resumable handoff attempt, key rotation, restart recovery, result response loss
  and cleanup ordering.

Verification:

- `cargo test -p sagnir-store`
- payload-staging/publication/handoff state-machine model;
- platform durability, process-kill and path-substitution integration suite;
- non-resumable admission and quota/privacy tests.

Exit criteria:

- No ordinary handoff exists before complete encrypted immutable payload material,
  quota charge and file/parent-directory durability are verified.
- Caller/process loss cannot make an admitted handoff payload unavailable under its
  declared recovery horizon.
- Non-resumable tickets never own next-writer priority and can only wait or follow
  independently authorized cancellation/debt semantics.

### v0.111.46 - Emergency Writer Incarnation Recovery

Goal: recover security-critical journal authority from a permanently blocked or
compromised writer handoff without pretending the action is normal scheduling.

Deliverables:

- canonical `EmergencyWriterIncarnationRecovery` requires explicit operator
  confirmation plus threshold recovery authority independent of the blocked
  principal, admission-ledger keys and potentially compromised journal keys;
  authority roots/recipients are bootstrap-bound, purpose-separated and rotatable;
- prepared transition binds exact daemon/journal/admission-ledger incarnations and
  roots, blocked ticket/handoff/staging/recovery-epoch commitments, writer-lease
  generation, the v0.111.54 provider-complete fenced operation-set commitment,
  journal/provider pending or ambiguous operations, recovery attempts/budgets,
  compromise/availability reason, policy epoch and random operation/idempotency key;
- state machine is `EmergencyPrepared -> OldIncarnationFenced ->
  NewIncarnationActivated -> Finalized`, with explicit `Ambiguous`, `Conflict` and
  `RecoveryRequired`; every step is externally anchored/witnessed under an emergency
  namespace unavailable to the normal writer scheduler;
- fencing permanently closes old writer/admission lease generations, provider
  operation namespaces and handoff execution authority before activating a new
  daemon/journal/admission-ledger incarnation or explicitly linked recovery epoch;
  old processes, restored disks and delayed callers cannot acquire a new lease;
- new genesis commits predecessor roots, fence transition, quarantined ambiguous
  operation set, retained priority/ticket evidence and new key/ledger/journal epochs;
  it may perform required compromise key rotation without trusting old journal keys;
- provider/journal results arriving after the fence are authenticated against the
  old operation/incarnation and quarantined as `LateFencedResult`; they never apply
  to new authority, complete a reused ticket or disappear, and require explicit
  v0.111.49 effect classification/governed bridge resolution if their semantic
  effect may have committed;
- fencing is not a terminal staged-material cleanup transition: v0.111.50 retains
  payload, decoder, keys and quota until the effect bridge and all authoritative
  result roots reach an authenticated terminal outcome;
- v0.111.58 keeps this authority-changing path inactive until v0.111.49/v0.111.50
  and v0.111.54-v0.111.56 close effect, completeness and custody semantics;
- abandoned ticket ID, FIFO sequence, grant/handoff generation, nonce domains and
  operation IDs remain permanently non-reusable; new incarnation counters/random
  identities cannot alias old or cloned state;
- concurrent emergency attempts use expected old-incarnation/fence CAS and threshold
  operation namespace; response loss reconciles exact external evidence, while
  same-old-root different transition, forged authority or split witnesses conflict;
- permanent audit evidence retains operator confirmation, threshold transcript,
  reason, old/new roots, ambiguous/late results, fence and all key/provider actions;
  rollback/backup/compaction cannot remove it or relabel emergency recovery as a
  normal cancellation/debt conversion;
- tests cover forged/insufficient emergency authority, compromised journal keys,
  late committed result, unknown provider outcome, response loss at every state,
  concurrent recovery, restored old process, fence rollback, new-genesis crash,
  ticket/operation reuse and subsequent compromise rotation.

Verification:

- `cargo test -p sagnir-crypto`
- `cargo test -p sagnir-store`
- emergency fence/incarnation/late-result state-machine model;
- threshold/witness/provider ambiguity and crash/rollback integration suite.

Exit criteria:

- Emergency recovery is an independently authorized, operator-confirmed authority
  transition, never a scheduler timeout or maintenance-priority bypass.
- Old handoff and every possible late result are permanently fenced/quarantined
  before a linked new incarnation can perform security-critical recovery.
- No old identifier, operation, lease or nonce authority becomes reusable, and
  permanent evidence survives rollback, backup and compaction.

### v0.111.47 - Retention Verification Accounting Epoch

Goal: bound routine retention/availability assurance without consuming emergency
cold-recovery capacity or accepting cross-purpose accounting artifacts.

Deliverables:

- canonical `RetentionVerificationAccountingEpoch` has a unique format kind,
  commitment domain, operation/replay namespace, purpose-specific encryption/HMAC
  key, policy epoch, predecessor root and refill/reset authority independent of
  anchor recovery, discovery, migration and handoff recovery accounting;
- checked persistent counters/limits cover challenge issuance, random samples,
  responses, tags/proof nodes, attestation verification, provider calls, network/
  response bytes, decode/hash/crypto work, concurrent operations, failures,
  temporary storage and current-process elapsed duration;
- every routine/activation verification reserves maximum work before execution;
  exact completion reconciles measured result, while timeout, response loss, crash
  or unknown provider execution retains conservative charge until idempotent query;
- per-process duration uses only one `ClockEpoch`; persistent counters, provider
  ambiguity and challenge IDs survive restart/clone, and reset/refill requires
  purpose-specific authority bound to prior root, consumed counters and policy/
  availability epochs rather than local wall time;
- v0.111.38 availability activation binds the exact consumed verification root,
  challenge/attestation result commitments and remaining policy capacity; rollback,
  stale root or partially accounted evidence cannot activate a class;
- periodic assurance and verification-only copy sampling/downloads use this epoch;
  actual cold recovery copy retrieval, decryption and restoration remain charged to
  `AnchorRecoveryAccountingEpoch`, with a typed boundary that prevents relabeling
  emergency restoration as routine verification;
- verifier/tag key work under v0.111.44 remains separately keyed but its resource
  use is charged here; retention-verification keys cannot decrypt recovery copies,
  reset recovery budgets or authorize proof/tag migration;
- canonical vectors reject substitution among retention verification, anchor
  recovery, bootstrap discovery, journal migration and handoff recovery keys,
  counters, resets, checkpoints, receipts, challenge IDs and operation evidence;
- unavailable/wrong accounting key or authority fails closed while retaining exact
  pending assurance state; it does not initialize fresh counters, consume cold-
  recovery capacity or silently downgrade protected availability;
- v0.111.53 is the mandatory provisioning/descriptor/rotation/retirement closure;
  this milestone cannot activate a live protected assurance epoch independently;
- tests cover periodic/activation challenge limits, provider response loss, restart/
  clone, cross-`ClockEpoch`, reset replay, key rotation/loss, activation with stale
  root, cross-domain substitution and actual-recovery-versus-verification routing.

Verification:

- `cargo test -p sagnir-crypto`
- `cargo test -p sagnir-store`
- retention-verification accounting/cross-domain state-machine model;
- independent canonical vectors and restart/ambiguity integration suite.

Exit criteria:

- Routine assurance never consumes or resets emergency cold-recovery accounting,
  and actual restoration cannot masquerade as periodic verification.
- Restart and provider ambiguity preserve conservative persistent charges under a
  purpose-specific key/root/reset authority.
- Availability activation binds one exact fully accounted verification root.

### v0.111.48 - Recovery Discovery Metadata Privacy Profiles

Goal: state and test what coordinators, endpoints and network observers learn from
bootstrap discovery, including limits of relay and cover-query mitigations.

Deliverables:

- each privacy profile has a network/provider leakage inventory for recovery timing/
  frequency, source network identity, coordinator/endpoint selection, query order,
  threshold participant identity/availability, retries/failures, response class,
  token-generation linkability, transport volume and coordinator/endpoint logs;
- baseline profile explicitly permits those observations and never claims opaque
  namespace/query keys hide frequency, source, endpoint choice or token linkage;
- padded profile defines canonical authenticated response size classes and coarse
  result kinds for latest-value, absence, refusal, invalid authority and conflict;
  padding/coarsening cannot turn a partial/ambiguous result into valid authority;
- response processing and emission use bounded policy-defined timing classes with
  fixed minimum work/jitter where feasible so valid/invalid/absence/refusal does not
  create a trivial fast/slow oracle; network/provider scheduling outside the control
  boundary remains a documented leakage and no constant-time network claim is made;
- provider/coordinator logs use opaque token/operation commitments, fixed/coarse
  result/timing buckets, strict access controls and declared retention/redaction/
  deletion horizons; plaintext tokens, daemon identity, threshold secrets, exact
  recovery roots and sensitive endpoint topology never enter general logs;
- protected profile may require an admitted authenticated relay set, source-
  separation transport and fixed-cadence cover-query schedule with independent
  cover capacity; cover operations cannot consume, reserve, reset or reveal valid
  token discovery budgets and cannot be provider-selected to target real events;
- coordinators inherently learn requests reaching them and therefore recovery
  frequency/linkability unless v0.111.52 admits a blind protocol whose complete
  coordinator view satisfies its observer-specific bounds; measured network timing
  alone cannot hide legitimate authentication or budget access; relays may
  themselves learn source/timing, and
  single relay/coordinator, global passive observer and endpoint-collusion limits
  remain explicit rather than inheriting a stronger privacy claim;
- threshold authorization and v0.111.42 pre-auth gate preserve response-shape/
  timing privacy without allowing invalid-auth floods to consume legitimate budget;
  public abuse exhaustion may cause a declared availability/timing signal but no
  token-validity oracle or authenticated budget mutation;
- profile health records missed cover windows, relay failure, direct fallback,
  unpadded response, excessive timing class and log-policy violation as permanent
  historical degradation; protected profiles fail closed instead of silently
  connecting directly or dropping cover;
- trace tests compare valid, invalid, stolen-token, absence, refusal, conflict,
  threshold failure, endpoint retry and real/cover queries across source addresses,
  timing/size classes, coordinator/endpoint collusion, relay failure and log output.

Verification:

- `cargo test -p sagnir-crypto`
- `cargo test -p sagnir-store`
- discovery privacy-profile and cover-schedule state-machine model;
- network/provider trace distinguishability and log-retention integration suite.

Exit criteria:

- Every profile states exactly which recovery metadata each observer learns and
  never treats opaque namespaces as traffic-analysis protection.
- Protected relay/cover claims exist only when measured trace bounds and health are
  met; otherwise status preserves a permanent degraded interval or refuses.
- Response shaping and public-abuse isolation reduce validity oracles without
  changing recovery authority or legitimate discovery capacity.

### v0.111.49 - Post-Fence Ambiguous Effect Resolution

Goal: prevent an emergency fence from being mistaken for proof that an old
provider or journal operation did not commit immediately before fencing.

Deliverables:

- every fenced operation enters an authenticated append-only classification chain;
  v0.111.55 fixes its transition lifecycle and compensation semantics, while absence,
  timeout, local rollback, fence completion and missing provider state never imply
  `ProvenNoEffect`;
- derived `ProvenNoEffect` requires final non-acceptance plus reconciled pre-acceptance
  abort, accepted-never-started evidence or v0.111.82 started-no-effect evidence, bound
  to operation, old incarnation/namespace, fence and query roots;
  `Committed` binds the exact semantic effect, provider result and old roots;
- `EffectUnknown` carries the complete uncertainty set, affected authority scope,
  dependency/conflict closure, provider query/fence capabilities, retained staging
  commitment and permanent evidence root into the new incarnation;
- new-incarnation activation may restore unrelated authority, but every operation
  dependent on or conflicting with `EffectUnknown` remains read-only/blocked; it
  cannot be retried, compensated, promoted, compacted away or selected by arrival
  order while the effect classification is unresolved;
- one canonical threshold-governed `EmergencyEffectBridge` binds old/new
  incarnation and authority roots, exact operation/effect commitment, provider and
  journal evidence, dependency closure, policy epoch and prior bridge root;
- the bridge has only `AdoptCommittedEffect`, `AcceptProvenNonExecution` and
  `AuthorizeCompensation` outcomes; adoption creates a new signed transition that
  acknowledges the old effect, while compensation records both the original
  uncertainty/commitment and the independently authorized compensating effect;
- compensation never rewrites history or proves non-execution, and is allowed only
  when policy defines a bounded effect-specific compensator and residual-risk
  classification; irreversible or unbounded effects remain blocked;
- a late authenticated result can append a refinement after `EffectUnknown` only
  through the same bridge CAS under v0.111.55; conflicting results, bridge attempts
  or provider split views create permanent conflict evidence and preserve read-only
  authority;
- when provider semantics cannot conclusively query, fence and identify the effect,
  status reports `EffectUnknown` and the affected authority remains read-only rather
  than replaying the operation under a new ID or incarnation;
- tests cover commit immediately before fence, response loss, false non-execution,
  late result, provider split view, unrelated new-incarnation progress, dependent/
  conflicting refusal, adoption, compensation, irreversible effect and bridge CAS.

Verification:

- `cargo test -p sagnir-store`
- emergency-effect classification/bridge/dependency state-machine model;
- provider query/fence ambiguity and compensation integration suite;
- old/new incarnation canonical bridge vectors.

Exit criteria:

- Fencing proves only that future old-namespace execution is impossible; it never
  proves the immediately preceding effect absent.
- Unknown effects block every dependent or conflicting authority action across new-
  incarnation activation until one governed bridge outcome commits.
- No retry, adoption or compensation can erase uncertainty or apply one effect
  twice.

### v0.111.50 - Emergency Handoff Material Retirement

Goal: retain staged payload recovery material while an emergency effect is unknown,
then destroy it and release quota only after authenticated terminal resolution.

Deliverables:

- emergency fencing moves every affected staged generation to
  `EmergencyFencedPendingEffect`, preserving encrypted payload/reconstruction bytes,
  decoder/schema, wrappers/keys, staging commitment, retention policy and full quota;
- bridge outcomes map to `EmergencyResolvedAdopted`,
  `EmergencyResolvedNotExecuted`, `EmergencyResolvedCompensated` or the v0.111.59
  `EmergencyResolvedNormalized`; no scheduler, timeout, disconnect, local deletion
  or fence-only transition can create them;
- `EmergencyFencedPendingEffect` remains decryptable to admitted recovery authority
  for the declared emergency horizon, with renewal/abandonment governed by
  v0.111.56, and cannot execute, become a new handoff, satisfy a different operation
  or lose its conservative resource charge;
- terminal cleanup first reconciles journal, admission ledger, provider result,
  bridge and dependency roots, then authenticates deletion of every managed staged
  encryption instance and wrapper able to recover its DEK before releasing physical
  capacity or retiring decoder/key support;
- normalized cleanup preserves `ExecutionKnowledge::Unknown`, requires the exact
  normalization receipt and keeps every history-sensitive dependency blocked under
  v0.111.59 even when admitted current-state dependencies reopen; v0.111.67 prepares
  the composite-head CAS and v0.111.68 requires external reconciliation plus local
  activation before cleanup or reopening;
- staged generations use independently accountable keys/encryption instances; a
  shared wrapping key cannot be destroyed while another live or unresolved staged
  payload depends on it, and wrapper deletion alone is not called payload erasure;
- immutable permanent evidence retains encrypted-payload commitment, operation and
  old/new incarnation roots, effect classification, bridge outcome, cleanup/
  destruction evidence, residual copies and quota-release transition after bytes
  are gone, without retaining plaintext, raw digest or a membership oracle;
- managed backup/snapshot copies follow declared retirement and destruction
  semantics; unknown or unmanaged copies remain explicit residual exposure and
  cannot support a completed physical-erasure claim;
- cleanup crash/restart is idempotent and forward-only; ambiguous provider deletion
  remains charged and recoverable, while missing key/material before terminal
  resolution enters permanent `RecoveryRequired` rather than releasing quota;
- v0.111.56 supplies realm/global custody bounds, immutable decoder retention,
  authoritative renewal and permanent fail-closed `AbandonedUnresolved`; this
  milestone alone cannot retain unresolved material without a bounded custody plan;
- tests cover fence at each staging/execute/result boundary, all bridge outcomes,
  key rotation, shared wrappers, backup copies, partial deletion, response loss,
  restart, duplicate cleanup and quota release before evidence reconciliation.

Verification:

- `cargo test -p sagnir-crypto`
- `cargo test -p sagnir-store`
- emergency staged-material retention/retirement state-machine model;
- key/wrapper/destruction/quota crash and backup integration suite.

Exit criteria:

- Unknown effects retain the exact staged recovery material, keys, decoder and quota
  required for governed resolution.
- Only an authenticated terminal bridge outcome plus complete reconciliation permits
  staged-byte destruction and corresponding capacity release.
- Permanent commitments and resolution evidence survive cleanup without preserving
  recoverable plaintext by accident.

### v0.111.51 - Handoff Staging Traffic Privacy Composition

Goal: make encrypted handoff staging a measured consumer of the daemon and storage-
observer traffic profiles rather than treating opaque payload files as invisible.

Deliverables:

- v0.111.45/v0.111.50 staging and cleanup are named consumers of the v0.111.18 and
  v0.111.29 profiles, with canonical baseline, padded and high-security profile IDs
  and parameters committed inside the private realm descriptor;
- baseline declares observable staging/retention/cleanup timing, allocation and
  padded-size class, retry cadence, filesystem timestamps, directory churn, changed
  backup/CoW blocks, emergency retention duration and final destruction activity;
- exact plaintext length, plaintext digest and reconstruction metadata exist only
  inside the encrypted authenticated record; any external equality/integrity
  commitment is purpose-keyed, epoch-bound and domain-separated, never a raw stable
  plaintext digest or cross-realm/cross-operation membership oracle;
- padded profile uses admitted immutable size classes, preallocation, timestamp
  normalization and bounded publication/cleanup windows; oversize payloads fail or
  enter a declared larger class without silently exposing exact length;
- high-security profile uses a measured fixed-cadence staging arena or equivalent
  cover-staging schedule for create/write/sync/publish/retain/cleanup classes;
  cover entries consume independent capacity and cryptographic work but can never
  become a handoff, effect, result, recovery payload or legitimate quota receipt;
- emergency fence, prolonged pending-effect retention, bridge resolution and cleanup
  participate in the same observer trace contract; unavoidable retention-duration
  leakage is declared, and cover work cannot claim to hide an unbounded event;
- missed windows, unplanned allocation, distinguishable retry, timestamp failure,
  backup-padding failure, emergency cleanup outside schedule or cover exhaustion
  permanently degrades the exact historical profile interval;
- trace accounting includes staged bytes, arenas, wrappers, ambiguous generations,
  cover work, backup-block churn and terminal cleanup; capacity pressure never
  triggers an undeclared privacy downgrade or premature emergency deletion;
- tests compare resumable/non-resumable, real/cover, success/failure, disconnect,
  ordinary/emergency retention, all bridge outcomes, restart and cleanup traces
  across filesystem, block, snapshot, backup and provider observers.

Verification:

- `cargo test -p sagnir-store`
- handoff traffic-profile/arena/health state-machine model;
- filesystem/block/snapshot/backup distinguishability suite;
- padding, fixed-cadence and emergency-retention overhead benchmarks.

Exit criteria:

- Every profile states exactly what handoff staging and emergency cleanup leak to
  each admitted storage observer.
- Raw plaintext length/digest never appears outside authenticated encryption, and
  external commitments cannot correlate payloads across protected domains.
- High-security claims hold for measured staging/retention/cleanup traces or the
  affected interval remains permanently degraded.

### v0.111.52 - Discovery Cover Observer Boundary

Goal: scope cover-query privacy to observers the selected protocol can actually
hide recovery occurrence from, including an active coordinator.

Deliverables:

- each discovery profile contains an observer/claim matrix for source network,
  relay, coordinator, selected/non-selected endpoint, threshold participant,
  provider operator, local host, backup/log reader and global passive observer;
- the baseline and coordinator-visible cover profiles explicitly place a
  coordinator that authenticates requests or accesses legitimate accounting
  outside the recovery-occurrence privacy claim, even when network size/timing and
  endpoint traces for real/cover requests match;
- coordinator-visible cover uses a separate purpose-keyed cover-accounting domain
  with the same externally observable operation/response shape, but documents any
  distinction available after authentication, authorization or real-budget access;
  cover accounting cannot consume, reserve, reset or infer legitimate capacity;
- a profile may include the coordinator in its privacy claim only through a reviewed
  oblivious/blind protocol whose coordinator view cannot distinguish real and cover
  slots by token, authentication path, budget access, query data, response handling,
  logs or errors; timing similarity alone is never sufficient;
- blind-profile transcripts bind slot/profile/relay set and authenticated outcome
  without granting cover operations recovery authority; malformed or exhausted
  blind slots consume separate abuse/cover capacity and cannot mutate real budgets;
- colluding observers receive only the intersection of claims supported by the
  profile; single-coordinator, relay/coordinator, coordinator/endpoint and global-
  observer limits are explicit, and no topology label silently implies anonymity;
- health and status identify which observer claim degraded when direct fallback,
  distinguishable coordinator accounting, blind-protocol failure, missed cover slot
  or log-policy violation occurs; protected policy refuses unsupported claims;
- active-coordinator trace tests inspect authentication path, key/token use,
  accounting namespace, provider calls, response class and logs in addition to
  network timing/size, with real/cover/invalid/absent/refused/conflict cases.

Verification:

- `cargo test -p sagnir-crypto`
- `cargo test -p sagnir-store`
- observer-claim/cover-accounting/blind-profile state-machine model;
- active coordinator, relay, endpoint and collusion trace suite.

Exit criteria:

- A coordinator that can observe legitimate authentication or budget access is
  explicitly outside the occurrence-privacy claim.
- Coordinator-private cover is claimed only for a reviewed protocol whose complete
  coordinator view is measured and indistinguishable within declared bounds.
- Separate cover accounting preserves operation shape without becoming recovery
  authority or consuming legitimate discovery capacity.

### v0.111.53 - Retention Accounting Key Lifecycle

Goal: make retention-verification accounting key provisioning, publication,
rotation and retirement explicit before any assurance epoch becomes authoritative.

Deliverables:

- initial provisioning reserves provider/profile capacity and durable operation ID,
  creates/wraps a purpose-separated key, queries ambiguous execution, verifies exact
  key purpose/suite/store/realm/epoch and usability, and commits the reconciled
  provider result before an accounting root or assurance challenge can activate;
- the private realm descriptor binds the authenticated key descriptor, provider/
  capability mode, recipients, suite, epoch, accounting-root genesis commitment and
  independent recovery/rotation authority; no bootstrap/public metadata exposes it;
- state machine is `KeyReserved -> ProviderPending -> KeyVerified ->
  DescriptorPrepared -> AccountingActivated`, with `Ambiguous`, `Conflict`,
  `Unavailable` and `RecoveryRequired`; restart never regenerates or assumes absence;
- rotation is copy-on-write: provision and verify the new key, prepare successor
  descriptor/accounting epoch, atomically activate both under expected old roots,
  and preserve old key/descriptor/checkpoints while any challenge, provider response,
  pending accounting reservation, reconciliation, backup or rollback horizon uses it;
- ambiguous provider key creation, wrapping, rotation or destruction uses the normal
  provider-operation reconciliation state machine and remains capacity-charged;
  local timeout never reprovisions, adopts an unknown handle or retires the old key;
- retirement proves no live/pending accounting root or verification transcript needs
  the old epoch, commits destruction intent/result where policy requires it, and
  requires the signed v0.111.57 final epoch closure before destruction while
  preserving canonical historical verification evidence without retaining excess
  operational authority;
- key loss/unavailability blocks new assurance and protected activation while
  preserving prior evidence and conservative pending charges; it never resets
  counters, falls back to another key purpose or consumes recovery accounting;
- tests cover first provisioning, provider ambiguity/conflict, descriptor switch,
  challenge/response during rotation, response loss, restart/clone, old-root rollback,
  premature retirement, key loss, backup restore and cross-purpose substitution.

Verification:

- `cargo test -p sagnir-crypto`
- `cargo test -p sagnir-store`
- retention-accounting key lifecycle/descriptor state-machine model;
- provider ambiguity, copy-on-write rotation and retirement integration suite.

Exit criteria:

- No assurance epoch activates before its exact key is capacity-reserved, reconciled,
  verified and committed into the private descriptor.
- Rotation cannot strand pending verification/accounting work or create two writable
  accounting epochs.
- Provider ambiguity and key loss preserve capacity, counters and evidence without
  regeneration, fallback or cross-purpose budget reset.
- v0.111.58 keeps protected retention accounting inactive until v0.111.57 historical
  closure is implemented for the selected key/provider profile.

### v0.111.54 - Atomic Provider Namespace Fence Completeness

Goal: prove an emergency fence covers every operation accepted by the old provider
namespace, including operations a compromised writer omitted from its local journal.

Deliverables:

- canonical `ProviderNamespaceFenceReceipt` binds provider/service identity and
  capability profile, old namespace/lease/incarnation, atomic fence generation,
  provider consistency mode, final accepted-operation sequence/high-watermark,
  v0.111.61 append-log/map roots, operation count and predecessor receipt/root;
- fence activation and accepted-operation snapshot share one provider linearization
  point: every operation is either rejected after that point or included in the
  final sequence/root; no response timing, local process ordering or later inventory
  query can create a gap between snapshot and fence;
- the provider returns a complete authenticated bounded operation inventory, or
  inclusion/range/non-inclusion proofs against the final accumulator with resumable
  pagination whose page/frontier continuity proves complete coverage;
- each accepted entry binds operation ID, idempotency key, effect class/commitment
  when known, submission/result state, lease/incarnation and provider sequence;
  opaque entries remain classified as unknown rather than omitted;
- provider capability admission declares linearizability/consistency assumptions,
  accumulator/proof suite, maximum inventory/proof work, query authorization and
  retention horizon for final receipts and negative-operation proofs;
- completeness assurance is conditional on the selected v0.111.60 profile; a
  provider-signed root alone is only `ProviderAssertedComplete` and cannot detect a
  provider/writer pair that consistently omits an accepted operation;
- local reconciliation compares journal/admission-ledger/provider sets against the
  final root; provider-accepted operations absent locally become durable
  `ProviderAcceptedLocalUnknown` records before any new-incarnation authority opens;
- local-only operations absent from the provider root remain non-execution candidates
  only when a retained v0.111.61 authenticated-map non-inclusion proof covers their
  exact ID, namespace and fence generation and its map/log projection proof is valid;
